title: Limits of Prompt-Based Engineering
concept: Prompting is fast but vulnerable to format drift, context limits, and token-cost constraints, requiring structured evaluation.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes prompt-based engineering?

options.text: Very fast to set up, but harder to control at scale
Slow to set up, but perfectly stable forever
Free to run no matter how many tokens you use

exercises.explain: Prompting is incredibly fast to start with, but maintaining it at scale brings unexpected challenges.

cards.title: The Limits of Prompts
cards.body: Prompting an AI is like magic—you type a few sentences, and it builds a chatbot! But what happens when your app grows, the bills skyrocket, or the AI suddenly changes how it answers? Let's explore the hidden limits of relying solely on prompts.

cards.title: Prompt-Based Engineering Limits
cards.body: Prompt-Based Engineering is building AI applications using only written instructions (prompts). While incredibly fast to build, it faces hard limits: prompts can suddenly stop working when models update (format drift), struggle with massive amounts of data (context limits), and get very expensive to run (token costs).

cards.title: The Phone Directions Analogy
cards.body: Imagine prompting as giving directions to a driver over the phone. It is fast and gets them moving instantly! But if the drive is 10 hours long (context limit) or the phone connection gets static-y (format drift), the driver might get lost. You eventually need a GPS map (structured evaluation) instead of just yelling directions.

cards.title: When Prompts Drift
cards.body: A travel app uses a prompt to extract flight details into a list. One day, the AI provider updates the model. Suddenly, the AI starts writing 'Flight:' before each item, breaking the app's code. This is a classic example of 'format drift' breaking a prompt-reliant system.

cards.title: The Permanent Prompt Myth
cards.body: Myth: Once a prompt works perfectly in your playground, it will work forever in your app. Reality: AI models update behind the scenes. Slight updates can cause the AI to format its answers differently, causing unexpected crashes in your software.

cards.title: Prototype vs. Production
cards.body: Let's compare the differences between starting out with prompts versus maintaining them at scale.

left.text: Fast setups, instant changes, low starting effort.

right.text: Format drift, context limits, and high token costs.

exercises.prompt: What is a key risk of relying entirely on prompt engineering for production apps?

options.text: They can be vulnerable to format drift and rising token costs.
They require writing complex python code to start.
They never run out of memory or context space.
They guarantee the exact same output format forever.

exercises.explain: Prompts are great for quick prototyping, but they are vulnerable to format drift, context limits, and rising token costs over time.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Writing the perfect prompt solves all issues with context limits and token costs.
exercises.explain: Even with perfect instructions, prompts can fail due to model updates, context size limitations, or high token fees.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the term that describes when an AI model changes its output structure and breaks code.
exercises.sentence: When an AI suddenly changes its response layout, it is called format drift.
exercises.explain: Format drift occurs when an AI changes the way it structures its responses, which can break the code reading those responses.

exercises.prompt: You prompt an AI to return only the capital city name. Which output shows format drift?

options.text: Output B: 'Sure, here is your capital: Paris'

exercises.explain: Output B shows format drift because it added extra conversational text ('Sure, here is your capital:') instead of returning just the requested clean data.

exercises.prompt: Your app is growing and users are reporting that the AI occasionally outputs broken JSON. How should you address this limit of prompt engineering?

options.text: Prompting has no limits and scales perfectly without tests.
You should write a longer prompt to fix all future drift issues.
Implement structured, automated evaluation to catch format drift and monitor costs.
Only use prompts when you have an unlimited budget and zero users.

exercises.explain: Relying only on casual manual checks (like reading 3 outputs) fails to catch subtle format changes or costly token hikes when your app scales up. Automated structured evaluation is necessary to monitor performance reliably.
